Fern Polrrrcal PRACTTcEs Covrl,rrssroN
428 J Street . Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
June 12,2007
Kris Miller Fisher
2200 State Street
Santa Barbara, California 93 1 05-3558
RE: Your Request for Informal
Our File No. I-07-082
Dear Ms. Fisher:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the political Reform Act (the ..Act,).r !ir." fou seek g"r"rut
guidance rather than advice o,n a specific, pending governmental decision, wJprovide
you with informal assistance.z Also, please note thit our advice is based solely on the
provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application , if any,of other
conflict-of-interest laws such as common law Conflict of interest or Government Code
Section 1090.
QUESTION
As an executive staff assistant to a county supervisor, do you have a conflict of
interest in participating in govemment decisions involving your county and the
University of Califomia, Santa Barbarawhile your husband works foittre University?
CONCLUSION
No, under the limited facts you have provided you do not have a conflict of
interest in participating in governmental deciiions between your county and the
University of Califomia, Santa Barbarabecause your economic interesi in your husband,s
income falls under the Act's "government salary exception.,,
FACTS
You are the executive assistant to the Santa BarbaraCounty Supervisor. you
I GovemmentCode sections 81000-91014. Commissionregulations appear attitle2,sections
18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.
2Informal assistance does not off.i th" immunityprovided by a commission opinion or formal
written advice. (Regulation I 8329(cX3).)
ffi=


File No. l-07-082
Page No. 2
have responsibilities that involve working with the University of California, Santa
Barbara (UCSB) and the adjacent community of Isla Vista, which serves many of
UCSB's students as well as some faculty. Your husband, Marc Fisher, is Associate Vice
Chancellor for Campus Design Facilities at UCSB. Some of the governmental decisions
in which you are involved could concern UCSB.
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interest of persons who have supported them. (Section 8l00l0).)
Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits anypublic official from making,participating in
making or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the
meaning of the Act, when it is "reasonably foreseeable" thatthe governmental decision
will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic
interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 1S700(a).) In order to determine whether a public
official has a disqualifiring conflict of interest in a given govemmental decision, the
Commission has adopted an eight-step analylical framework. (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-
(8).) The general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public
official makes a govefllmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on one or more of his or her financial interests. (Section 87103.)
Step One: Are You a Public Official?
The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act apply only to public officials.
(Section 87100.) Section 82048 defines public offrcial as every member, officer,
employee or consultant of a state or local govemment agency. As the executive assistant
to the Santa Barbaru County Supervisor you are an employee of a local government
agency, thus you are a public official.
Step Two: will You Be Making, Participating in Making, or rnfluencing a
Governmental Decision?
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the purview of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.)
In addition, a public official "participates in making" a governmental decision
when he or she, without substantive review, negotiates, advises, or makes
recommendations regarding a decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) You will be participating


File No. I-07-082
Page No. 3
in making a goveflrmental decision if you advise, recommend or negotiate a course of
action for the County.'
Furthermore, a public official is "influencing a governmental decision" if he or
she contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence any member, officer,
employee, or consultant of the government agency regarding the decisions. (Regulation
L8702.3) Therefore, you would be influencing a governmental decision if you contact,
appear before, or otherwise attempt to influence any member, officer, employee, or
consultant of the Santa Barbara County Supervisor's Office regarding governmental
decisions.
Step Three: What Are Your Economic Interests?
A public official has a "financial interest" in agovernmental decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have amateial financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or
her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests. (Section
87103; Regulations 18703-18703.5.) The applicable economic interests include:
An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $ 2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation
18703.1(a)), or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d);
Regulation 1 8703. I (b));
a
a
a
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or
indirect interest of $ 2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation
18703.2);
An economic interest in any source of income, including promised
income, which aggregates to $500 or more within r2 months prior to the
decision (Section 87103(c); Regulation 1 8703.3);
An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts
aggregate to $390 or more within l2 months prior to the decision (section
871 03(e); Regulation 187 03 .4);
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediaie
family. This is also knows as the "personal financial effects" rure.
(Section 87 103 ; Regulation I 8703.5.)
a
a
3 Exception: Making or participating in a govemmental decision does not include appearances by a
public official as a member of the general public before an agency in the course of its prescribed
governmental function to represent himself or herself on matters related solely to the official,s wholly
owned real properLy or business entity. (See Regulation 18702.4)


File No. I-07-082
Page No. 4
Economic Interests Identified
Source of Income: Your question focuses on potential conflicts arising from your
husband's employment as Associate Vice Chancellor for Campus Design Facilities at
UCSB. As noted above, a public official has an economic interest in his or her own
income, (Section 87103; Regulation 13703.5) and in the source of that income. (Section
87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.) Income includes any community property interest in the
income of a spouse. (Section 82030.)
Because your husband is employed by the University of Califomia, you have an
economic interest in his income and the source of his income, as you have a community
property share in his income. (Section 82030.) However, the Act's definition of income
expressly excludes "salary ...received from a state, local, or federal government
agency...;' (Section 82030(b)(2), copy enclosed.) Because UCSB iJa state government
agency under Section 82041, the salary paid to your husband is not consideri ..income,,
under Section 82030(bX2). The governmental salary exception also covers employment-
related benefits, such as pensions (In re Moore (1977) r rppc ops. 33; also see
Regulation 18232(a)), and provisions of collective bargaining agreements concerning
work days and hours (Sylvia Advice Letter, No. I-02-176).
Thus, under the "governmental salary''exception, you do not have an economic
interest in UCSB as a source of income.
Personal Finances: Every public official has an economic interest in his or her personal
finances and those of his or her immediate family. (Section 87|}3;Regulation lSZO:.S.;
A governmental decision will have an effect on this economic interest if the decision wiil
result in your or youl immediate family's personal expenses, income, assets or liabilities,
other than the financial affect on your real property interest or business, increasing or
decreasing. (Ibid.)
However, you have not provided any other facts indicating your participation in
any govemment decision would affect your personal finances in any way otheithan those
already discussed above, therefore we do not further analyze your personal finances as a
potential conflict of interest. Should you need further assistance o, thi. issue with
respect to a specific govemmental decision please feel free to write agun.


File No. I-07-082
Page No. 5
If you have other questions on this matter,please contact me at (916) 322-5660
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
A,fdruLhl.W$,,'-
By: Sukhi K. Brar
Counsel, Legal Division
SKBjgl
I
